paper: POLICYGUARD: A Dialogue-Grounded Sub-Agent Verifier for Policy Adherence in LLM Agents

research_equation.target: Achieve reliable policy adherence for LLM agents executing customer-facing tool calls
research_equation.formulation: Given a company policy P (natural language document), an agent A with dialogue history H executing mutating tool call T, ensure that T is blocked if any procedural precondition in P is unmet, OR passed with confidence that all preconditions are satisfied.
research_equation.key_tension: Prior work treats policy adherence as a safeguarding problem (argument-level checks on tool calls). However, ~67% of real policy requirements are process-level (dialogue-dependent), requiring conversation-awareness, self-reasoning over NL policy text, and actionable remediation — three capabilities no prior system combines.

core_challenge.name: Process-level policy verification
core_challenge.description: Company policies require verifying speech acts (user consent, identity confirmation, insurance offer/response) and prior read-only tool results — predicates over open-ended dialogue, not tool arguments.
core_challenge.difficulty: Cannot be solved by argument-level precompiled predicates; requires natural language understanding of dialogue context.

sub_challenges.name: Conversation-awareness
sub_challenges.description: The verifier must have access to the full agent-user dialogue, not just the current tool call's arguments. Prior argument-level guards (TOOLGUARD, SOLVER-AIDED) structurally cannot access chat_history.
sub_challenges.module_in_paper: Verifier prompt includes formatted message history; read-only calls bypass verifier, mutating calls evaluated with full dialogue context.
sub_challenges.evaluation: PG-RAW-TRAJ ablation: stripping dialogue collapses Mut PASS1 from 0.260 to 0.000 — causal proof that dialogue is necessary.

sub_challenges.name: Self-reasoning over NL policy
sub_challenges.description: The verifier must read and reason over the raw natural-language policy document in context, rather than relying on precompiled predicates, keyword lists, or DSL translations.
sub_challenges.module_in_paper: Verifier prompt includes raw policy text as authoritative source; per-tool YAML checklist is advisory. Two key instructions enforce self-reasoning: 'Only trust values confirmed by tool results' and 'If a required action was never performed, treat it as NOT MET.'
sub_challenges.evaluation: PG-CHECKLIST-ONLY ablation: stripping raw policy raises block rate by 16 pp and drops Mut PASS1 by 15.4 pp — policy text provides real augmentation.

sub_challenges.name: Behavior-driving remediation
sub_challenges.description: A block alone is insufficient — the verifier must provide conversation-specific feedback that tells the agent what is missing and what to do next, enabling recovery within the same trial.
sub_challenges.module_in_paper: On BLOCK, verifier returns 1-3 sentence AGENT_MESSAGE grounded in the conversation (e.g., 'offer insurance, then ask the user to confirm the full booking summary'). Orchestrator pops the assistant turn and routes a ToolMessage(error) with remediation back to agent.
sub_challenges.evaluation: Two mechanisms compose: per-trial lift (remediation recovers same trial) and cross-trial consistency (same checks triggered reliably). Remediation typically resolves in a single corrective turn (Table 8).

sub_challenges.name: Checklist generation fidelity
sub_challenges.description: Converting raw policy into structured per-tool checklists without human authoring, minimizing hallucination/omission (~5-10% even with reviewer pass).
sub_challenges.module_in_paper: 4-step LLM pipeline: (1) tool classification, (2) per-tool YAML, (3) general rules, (4) reviewer pass. GPT 5.4 generates once, reused across vendors.
sub_challenges.evaluation: Reviewer catch rate ~5-10%. Gemini 2.5 Pro reads checklist more literally, costing Mut PASS4 — suggesting per-agent regeneration would close the gap.

sub_challenges.name: Cost-efficient gating
sub_challenges.description: Verifier calls add compute cost and latency. Must minimize overhead while maintaining coverage. Only mutating tool calls should trigger verification; read-only calls bypass.
sub_challenges.module_in_paper: Read-only calls bypass verifier (zero cost). Mini verifier ablation (GPT 5.4-mini) achieves ~13% cost saving with only -6 pp PASS4 drop. Total measured spend ~$680 for all experiments.
sub_challenges.evaluation: Verifier task is structurally lighter than agent task; tolerates substrate downgrade well. Overhead vs. baseline: $0.35-$0.80 per trial.

sub_challenges.name: Generalization across agent vendors
sub_challenges.description: The verifier must work across different frontier LLM agents (GPT, Claude, Gemini) without per-vendor tuning of the policy specification.
sub_challenges.module_in_paper: Single checklist (GPT 5.4-generated) reused across all three agents. Paired-verifier protocol isolates method contribution from substrate strength.
sub_challenges.evaluation: PG-CHECKLIST lifts PASS4 on all three vendors. However, Gemini reads checklist more literally. Per-agent regeneration likely closes the gap.

challenge_to_module_map.key_result: MUT PASS1 collapse 0.260 → 0.000; dialogue is causal
+15.4 pp Mut PASS1, -16.0 pp Blk% from adding raw policy
Remediation resolves in 1 turn; NMR below baseline on all agents

primary_modules: 4-step LLM pipeline (tool class → YAML → general rules → reviewer)
experiments: Table 2 main results (checklist reused across vendors)
challenge_to_module_map.key_result: ~5-10% hallucination/omission caught by reviewer; cross-vendor gap suggests per-agent regeneration
$0.35-$0.80 overhead/trial; mini verifier still +6 pp PASS4
Lifts all three; Gemini gap suggests regeneration improvement

research_lens_analysis.paradigm_shift_claimed: From safeguarding (argument-level, adversarial assumption) to policy adherence (process-level, honest-party assumption). The paper argues this is a distinct problem class requiring fundamentally different capabilities.
boundary_conditions: Evaluated only on τ²-BENCH airline (50 tasks) — the only domain with proper written policy + multi-turn dialogue prerequisites
Honest-user assumption: no adversarial user or agent (though adversarial robustness probed as a bonus)
English-language, customer-service domain (generalization to clinical/legal/NL-other unstudied)
Mutating tool calls only; read-only calls and verbal commitments not gated

key_design_tradeoffs.tension: Paired verifier (same vendor/tier) vs. independent verifier
key_design_tradeoffs.choice: Paired protocol to isolate method contribution from substrate strength. Accepts cross-vendor attribution confidence but doesn't test heterogeneous setups.

key_design_tradeoffs.tension: Advisory checklist + raw policy vs. strict checklist-only
key_design_tradeoffs.choice: Advisory regime (PG-CHECKLIST) chosen as headline — achieves best balance of PV recall and low block rate at the cost of not forcing strict compliance.

key_design_tradeoffs.tension: Pre-execution gating on mutating calls only vs. per-turn or verbal-commitment gating
key_design_tradeoffs.choice: Mutating-only gate minimizes cost and latency but leaves verbal commitments unmonitored.

key_design_tradeoffs.tension: Single checklist generated once vs. per-vendor regeneration
key_design_tradeoffs.choice: Fixed substrate for experimental attribution; accepts a measurable cost on Gemini 2.5 Pro Mut PASS4.
